standard_description: Partition shapes into parts with equal areas. Express the area of each part as a unit fraction of the whole. For example, partition a shape into 4 parts with equal area, and describe the area of each part as 1/4 of the area of the shape.
sources: NCTM Progressions for the Common Core State Standards in Mathematics — Geometry K–6, 2012
NCTM Progressions for the Common Core State Standards in Mathematics — Fractions 3–5, 2013
Van de Walle, J.A., Karp, K.S., & Bay-Williams, J.M. (2013). Elementary and Middle School Mathematics (8th ed.). Pearson.
Battista, M.T. (2007). The development of geometric and spatial thinking. NCTM Handbook.
EngageNY Grade 3 Module 7 Topic F — Shape Partition and Fractions, Teacher Edition, 2013
Smarter Balanced Assessment Consortium — Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications, 2015

misconceptions.short_label: Partitioning into visually similar but unequal parts
misconceptions.description: Student divides a shape into parts that LOOK similar but have unequal areas. Then labels each as the same unit fraction (e.g., 1/4) even though they are not equal.
misconceptions.example: Rectangle divided into 4 parts: top half is one part, bottom half divided into 3 unequal columns. Student labels all 4 as 1/4 each.
misconceptions.citation: NCTM Geometry Progressions K-6 (2012) p.12 — 'Children initially partition shapes into visually-similar but unequal parts, calling each a unit fraction of the whole; explicit attention to EQUAL AREA is required.'
misconceptions.remediation: Unit fraction (1/n) requires all n parts have EQUAL area. Check by overlaying or measuring. If parts differ, the labels are wrong. Use grid paper to verify equal areas.

misconceptions.short_label: Using wrong denominator for partitioned shape
misconceptions.description: Student counts the partitioned parts incorrectly when writing the unit fraction. E.g., counts 5 lines and writes 1/5 when the shape is divided into 6 parts.
misconceptions.example: Circle cut into 6 equal slices. Student labels each as 1/5 (miscounting slices, or confusing lines vs regions).
misconceptions.citation: NCTM Fractions Progressions 3-5 (2013) p.5 — 'The denominator of a unit fraction is the TOTAL number of equal parts, not the number of dividing lines or some other count; this distinction is non-trivial for children.'
misconceptions.remediation: Denominator = number of EQUAL PARTS the whole is divided into (not lines or vertices). Count parts: 1, 2, 3, …, n. Denominator = n.

misconceptions.short_label: Believing parts must be congruent (same shape), not just equal area
misconceptions.description: Student rejects partitions where parts have equal area but different shapes. E.g., a rectangle split into 4 differently-shaped parts of equal area.
misconceptions.example: Rectangle divided into a 1×4 strip and a 2×2 square (both area = 4). Student says it\'s not 'fair' because shapes differ.
misconceptions.citation: Battista (2007) p.870 — 'Equal-area partitions can use different shapes, but children often require congruence; teaching equal-area-by-counting-units (regardless of shape) is essential for 3.G.A.2.'
misconceptions.remediation: Equal AREA does not require equal SHAPE. Count unit squares in each part to verify equal area, even if shapes differ.

misconceptions.short_label: Confusing m/n with 1/n for multi-part shaded regions
misconceptions.description: For a shape with multiple shaded equal parts, student labels the shaded portion as 1/n (treating it as one piece) instead of m/n (m shaded out of n total).
misconceptions.example: Circle divided into 6 equal slices, 3 shaded. Student writes 1/6 instead of 3/6 (or 1/2).
misconceptions.citation: NCTM Fractions Progressions 3-5 (2013) p.7 — 'Children initially label any shaded fraction region as a unit fraction (1/n); recognizing that multiple shaded parts give m/n requires distinguishing the unit fraction (one part) from the total fraction (m parts).'
misconceptions.remediation: Numerator = number of shaded parts. Denominator = total parts. 3 shaded out of 6 = 3/6 (which equals 1/2). Practice counting shaded parts.

misconceptions.short_label: Believing only horizontal or vertical cuts work
misconceptions.description: Student believes only certain orientations of partition lines are valid (e.g., only horizontal or only vertical). Diagonal cuts are rejected.
misconceptions.example: Square divided diagonally into 2 equal triangles. Student says 'not fair — not split evenly' because the line isn\'t horizontal.
misconceptions.citation: Van de Walle et al. (2013) p.459 — 'Children often restrict valid partitions to horizontal or vertical cuts; diagonal cuts that produce equal areas must be explicitly modeled.'
misconceptions.source: Van de Walle Elementary and Middle School Mathematics
misconceptions.remediation: Any cut that produces equal areas is valid. Diagonal cuts work for squares, rectangles, etc. Demonstrate: fold a square along the diagonal — two equal triangles.

misconceptions.description: After partitioning, student thinks 1/8 is larger than 1/4 because '8 is bigger than 4.' Doesn\'t understand that more parts → smaller pieces.
misconceptions.example: Two same-size pizzas: one cut into 4 pieces, one into 8. Student says 1/8 piece is larger because '8 > 4.'
misconceptions.citation: NCTM Fractions Progressions 3-5 (2013) p.8 — 'A major Grade 3 misconception: larger denominator = larger piece. This contradicts the inverse relationship between denominator and piece size; concrete demonstration with paper folding resolves this.'
misconceptions.remediation: More parts = smaller pieces (assuming same whole). Cut two equal pizzas: one into 4, one into 8. Compare slice sizes. 1/8 < 1/4.

misconceptions.short_label: Believing the whole shape changes after partitioning
misconceptions.description: After dividing a shape into parts, student treats each part as a new 'whole,' losing sense of the original total.
misconceptions.example: Square divided into 4 quarters. 'What fraction is one quarter of the original square?' Student answers 1/1 (treats the quarter as a new whole) instead of 1/4.
misconceptions.citation: Battista (2007) p.871 — 'After partitioning, children may lose track of the original whole, treating each part as an independent unit; explicitly anchoring "the whole" before partitioning prevents this.'
misconceptions.remediation: Always anchor: 'The WHOLE is __' before partitioning. After partitioning, each part is a fraction of THAT whole, not of itself. Use color to keep whole boundary visible.
